sections.plain_summary: Trauma and PTSD (post-traumatic stress disorder) are conditions that develop after exposure to overwhelming experiences — such as abuse, accidents, violence, combat, medical trauma, or prolonged stress — that the nervous system could not fully process at the time. The result is a nervous system that stays stuck in a state of threat, replaying the past through flashbacks, nightmares, hypervigilance, and physical symptoms, even when the danger is long gone. PTSD is not a sign of weakness or a character flaw; it is a biological response to experiences that exceeded the capacity to cope.
sections.detailed_summary:
Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is a psychiatric and neurobiological condition that arises when the brain and nervous system fail to properly integrate a traumatic experience. During trauma, the brain's threat-detection system (the amygdala) becomes hyperactivated while the memory-consolidation and contextualisation functions of the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus are suppressed. This leaves traumatic memories stored in a fragmented, sensory-based form rather than as coherent narrative memories — meaning they can be involuntarily reactivated by cues that resemble the original event.

The resulting symptoms fall into four clusters recognised by DSM-5: intrusion symptoms (flashbacks, nightmares, intrusive thoughts), avoidance of reminders, negative alterations in mood and cognition, and hyperarousal (startle responses, sleep disruption, irritability, hypervigilance). Many people with PTSD also experience dissociation — a sense of unreality or disconnection from the body or environment.

Beyond the psychological symptoms, PTSD profoundly affects the body. Chronic dysregulation of the HPA (hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal) axis and the autonomic nervous system leads to altered cortisol patterns, persistent inflammation, gut disturbance, cardiovascular stress, and immune dysregulation. This is why people with PTSD often develop a wide range of physical health conditions.

Complex PTSD (C-PTSD) — increasingly recognised as a distinct diagnosis — describes the additional layers of difficulty that develop after prolonged or repeated trauma, particularly in childhood. It includes profound disruptions to self-identity, emotional regulation, and the capacity to form safe relationships, beyond the core PTSD cluster.

Diagnosis is clinical, based on structured interview and symptom questionnaires. Many people with PTSD go years without a correct diagnosis — especially when trauma history is not disclosed, or when physical symptoms are the presenting complaint. Effective trauma-focused treatments exist, but access and quality vary widely. Finding a practitioner trained specifically in trauma is often the critical step.

prevalence.summary: PTSD affects roughly 1 in 11 people at some point in their lives, though rates are substantially higher in people who have experienced certain types of trauma.
prevalence.estimate: Approximately 7–8% lifetime prevalence in the general population; point prevalence around 3–4%
prevalence.notes: Rates vary significantly by population and trauma type. Combat veterans, sexual assault survivors, and refugees show rates of 20–50%+. Women are diagnosed at roughly twice the rate of men, though this may partly reflect underdiagnosis in men and differences in trauma type. C-PTSD is not yet separately tracked in most epidemiological studies. Many cases go undiagnosed, particularly where trauma history is not explored by clinicians. Adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) studies suggest the cumulative burden of early trauma is far more widespread than clinical PTSD diagnoses capture.

subgroups.name: Acute stress disorder (ASD)
subgroups.description: A time-limited precursor to PTSD occurring within 4 weeks of a traumatic event. Many people who meet criteria for ASD go on to develop PTSD if untreated.
distinguishing_features: Onset within days to weeks of the traumatic event
Duration less than one month (if it persists, reassessment for PTSD is appropriate)
High levels of dissociation are common
Early intervention at this stage may prevent PTSD from consolidating

subgroups.name: Classic PTSD (single-event trauma)
subgroups.description: Develops after a discrete traumatic event such as an accident, assault, natural disaster, or medical emergency. Often more circumscribed, with clearer trauma triggers.
distinguishing_features: Identifiable onset linked to a specific event
Trauma triggers tend to be specific and recognisable
Generally responds well to trauma-focused therapies such as EMDR or prolonged exposure
Fewer disturbances in identity or relational functioning compared to C-PTSD

subgroups.name: Complex PTSD (C-PTSD)
subgroups.description: Develops after prolonged, repeated, or inescapable trauma — particularly childhood abuse, neglect, domestic violence, or captivity. Recognised as a distinct diagnosis in ICD-11, though not yet in DSM-5.
distinguishing_features: Core PTSD symptoms plus significant disturbances in emotional regulation
Deeply negative or unstable self-concept ('I am worthless', 'I am permanently damaged')
Difficulty in relationships — cycles of idealisation and collapse, fear of abandonment, difficulty with trust
Dissociative symptoms often more prominent and persistent
Higher rates of somatic symptoms and chronic pain
Often misdiagnosed as borderline personality disorder, bipolar disorder, or treatment-resistant depression

subgroups.name: Developmental / childhood trauma (ACEs)
subgroups.description: Trauma occurring during childhood development — before the brain and attachment system have fully formed — can have particularly wide-ranging effects on development, identity, biology, and adult health.
distinguishing_features: May not be recognised as 'trauma' by the person who experienced it ('that was just normal in our family')
Strong association with adult physical illness via ACE-score pathways (cardiovascular disease, autoimmune conditions, chronic pain)
Attachment disruption and disorganised attachment patterns in adult relationships
Often presents in adulthood as depression, anxiety, self-sabotage, or interpersonal difficulties rather than classic PTSD symptoms
Requires therapists trained in developmental or attachment-informed trauma approaches

subgroups.name: Medical / healthcare trauma
subgroups.description: PTSD triggered by frightening or dehumanising medical experiences — including ICU stays, cancer diagnosis, difficult childbirth, invasive procedures, or feeling dismissed or disbelieved by healthcare providers.
distinguishing_features: Avoidance of medical settings, which can prevent necessary care
Triggered by hospital environments, medical equipment, or clinical interactions
Often underrecognised by medical teams who focus on the physical illness
Particularly common in NICU parents, post-cardiac event survivors, and people who experienced traumatic birth

subgroups.name: Dissociative subtype of PTSD
subgroups.description: A distinct variant characterised by prominent depersonalisation (feeling detached from oneself) and derealisation (feeling the world is unreal), rather than hyperarousal.
distinguishing_features: Often presents with emotional numbing rather than intense distress or hyperarousal
May appear outwardly calm but is internally disconnected
Associated with more severe or prolonged trauma histories
May not respond as well to standard exposure-based therapies without first addressing the dissociation
Requires careful pacing in therapy to avoid destabilisation

symptoms.symptom: Intrusive flashbacks — involuntary reliving of the trauma as if it is happening now
symptoms.notes: Flashbacks can be visual, sensory, emotional, or bodily. They are triggered by cues that may not be consciously linked to the trauma, making them feel unpredictable and uncontrollable.

symptoms.symptom: Recurrent trauma-related nightmares
symptoms.notes: May be literal replays of the event or thematically related. Night terrors and thrashing during sleep are also reported. Sleep disruption reinforces daytime dysregulation.

symptoms.symptom: Hypervigilance — persistent sense of threat and scanning for danger
symptoms.notes: Often described as being unable to relax, always on guard, feeling unsafe in apparently neutral environments. Physically exhausting and can resemble anxiety.

symptoms.symptom: Exaggerated startle response
symptoms.notes: Disproportionate physical reaction to sudden sounds, movements, or touch. Reflects a nervous system calibrated to threat.

symptoms.symptom: Emotional numbing and disconnection
symptoms.notes: Difficulty feeling positive emotions, feeling cut off from others, reduced interest in life. Can be mistaken for depression.

symptoms.symptom: Avoidance of people, places, thoughts, or feelings that serve as reminders
symptoms.notes: Avoidance reduces short-term distress but prevents processing, maintaining and often widening the scope of PTSD over time.

symptoms.symptom: Dissociation — depersonalisation or derealisation
symptoms.notes: Feeling detached from one's body, watching oneself from outside, or experiencing the world as unreal or dreamlike. More prominent in complex or early-onset trauma.

symptoms.symptom: Negative beliefs about self and the world ('I am broken', 'nowhere is safe')
symptoms.notes: These beliefs often feel like objective reality rather than trauma-distorted thinking. Central to C-PTSD and closely linked to shame.

symptoms.symptom: Emotional dysregulation — rapid or extreme shifts in emotional state
symptoms.notes: Rage, panic, grief, or shame that arrives quickly and feels overwhelming. Particularly prominent in C-PTSD. Can be misinterpreted as personality disorder.

symptoms.symptom: Memory gaps and fragmented recall of the trauma
symptoms.notes: Trauma memories are often stored in sensory fragments rather than coherent narrative, making them hard to consciously recall but easy to be triggered by.

symptoms.symptom: Difficulty concentrating and cognitive fog
symptoms.notes: The hyperaroused or dissociated nervous system diverts resources from executive function. Often misread as ADHD or laziness.

symptoms.symptom: Persistent shame and guilt — often out of proportion to actual responsibility
symptoms.notes: Particularly common in survivors of interpersonal trauma and abuse. Shame is often a core organising feature of C-PTSD.

symptoms.symptom: Chronic pain — especially musculoskeletal, pelvic, or headache
symptoms.notes: Trauma is stored in the body. Chronic muscle tension, jaw clenching, pelvic holding patterns, and pain sensitisation are common. Often evaluated medically without trauma being considered.

symptoms.symptom: Gastrointestinal disturbance — nausea, bloating, IBS-like symptoms, gut pain
symptoms.notes: The gut-brain axis is profoundly affected by chronic stress and trauma. PTSD is significantly overrepresented in people with IBS and functional gut disorders.

symptoms.symptom: Sleep disturbance — difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or unrefreshing sleep
symptoms.notes: Beyond nightmares: racing thoughts at night, fear of sleeping, or sleeping too much as avoidance are all common patterns.

symptoms.symptom: Fatigue and exhaustion
symptoms.notes: Chronic nervous system activation is biologically expensive. Fatigue is often profound and may overlap with conditions such as ME/CFS.

symptoms.symptom: Heightened sensitivity to sound, light, touch, or other sensory input
symptoms.notes: A nervous system calibrated to threat may treat ordinary sensory input as a potential danger signal.

symptoms.symptom: Interpersonal difficulties — difficulty trusting others, relationship instability, fear of abandonment
symptoms.notes: Central to C-PTSD. Relationships may cycle through idealisation and collapse, or the person may avoid closeness altogether.

symptoms.symptom: Substance use as self-medication
symptoms.notes: Alcohol, cannabis, opioids, and other substances are commonly used to manage hyperarousal, emotional pain, or to induce sleep. Complicates and often delays treatment.

symptoms.symptom: Self-harm or suicidal ideation
symptoms.notes: Rates are significantly elevated in PTSD and C-PTSD. Self-harm may function as a coping mechanism for emotional dysregulation or dissociation. Requires direct assessment.

key_patterns: Persistent re-experiencing of a traumatic event through flashbacks, nightmares, or intrusive memories that feel vivid and present-tense
Active avoidance of reminders — people, places, sensations, topics — that cause significant restriction of life
Persistent negative changes in mood, thinking, or beliefs about self and world following trauma
Persistent hyperarousal or hypervigilance, with exaggerated startle response and sleep disruption
In C-PTSD: the above plus emotional dysregulation, profoundly negative self-concept, and persistent relational difficulties
Symptoms lasting more than one month and causing significant functional impairment
Pattern of repeated misdiagnosis (depression, anxiety, BPD, ADHD, somatic illness) without trauma history being explored

at_home.name: PCL-5 (PTSD Checklist for DSM-5)
at_home.description: A validated 20-item self-report questionnaire that maps directly onto DSM-5 PTSD symptom clusters. Freely available online. Scores of 33 or above suggest probable PTSD and warrant clinical assessment. Useful as a starting point for self-understanding and for bringing to a clinical appointment.
at_home.safety_notes: A questionnaire cannot diagnose PTSD — clinical interview by a trauma-trained professional is required. Completing the PCL-5 can itself be activating for some people; take breaks if needed and have support available.

at_home.name: IES-R (Impact of Event Scale – Revised)
at_home.description: A 22-item scale measuring intrusion, avoidance, and hyperarousal symptoms following a specific event. Widely used in research and clinical practice. Freely available online.
at_home.safety_notes: As with all trauma-related self-assessments, completing this can bring up distressing material. Treat results as informational, not diagnostic.

at_home.name: ACE (Adverse Childhood Experiences) questionnaire
at_home.description: A 10-item questionnaire assessing childhood exposure to abuse, neglect, and household dysfunction. Higher ACE scores are strongly associated with adult mental and physical health problems. Useful for understanding the role of early trauma when adult symptoms seem disproportionate to obvious triggers.
at_home.safety_notes: Reviewing childhood experiences can be emotionally difficult. This is best done when in a stable emotional state, ideally with therapeutic support in place.

at_home.name: Symptom tracking journal
at_home.description: Keeping a record of triggers, flashbacks, nightmares, dissociative episodes, sleep, mood, and physical symptoms over 2–4 weeks can help identify patterns and provide useful clinical information. Note what triggered each episode and the intensity of the response.
at_home.safety_notes: Detailed trauma journaling can be re-traumatising for some people. Focus on patterns and functional impact rather than reliving events in detail.

clinical.name: Structured clinical interview (CAPS-5 or SCID)
clinical.description: The Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale for DSM-5 (CAPS-5) is the gold-standard diagnostic tool — a detailed structured interview conducted by a trained clinician that evaluates each DSM-5 criterion. The SCID (Structured Clinical Interview for DSM Disorders) is also used. These assessments establish diagnosis, severity, and the nature of the traumatic events.
clinical.availability: Conducted by psychologists, psychiatrists, or specifically trained therapists in clinical or research settings. Not available in standard GP consultations. Trauma clinics and psychology services typically use these tools.

clinical.name: PCL-5 administered as clinical screening
clinical.description: The same questionnaire used for self-assessment can be administered in clinical settings to screen and monitor symptom severity over time.
clinical.availability: Should be available in any mental health or primary care setting, though not all clinicians routinely screen for PTSD.

clinical.name: International Trauma Questionnaire (ITQ) for C-PTSD
clinical.description: A validated measure specifically designed to assess both PTSD and C-PTSD according to ICD-11 criteria, capturing the additional domains of disturbances in self-organisation. Particularly useful where C-PTSD is suspected.
clinical.availability: Available in clinical psychology settings and some trauma-specialist services. Less widely known than the PCL-5.

clinical.name: Neurobiological and physiological markers (HRV, cortisol, inflammatory markers)
clinical.description: Research has consistently shown altered cortisol rhythms, reduced heart rate variability (HRV), and elevated inflammatory markers in PTSD. These are not yet part of routine clinical diagnosis but may be measured in research settings or functional medicine assessments and can help contextualise physical symptoms.
clinical.availability: Cortisol testing (salivary diurnal cortisol) available through functional medicine practitioners and some integrative clinicians. HRV measurable via wearable devices or clinical equipment.

clinical.name: Differential diagnosis assessment
clinical.description: Clinical evaluation to distinguish PTSD from bipolar disorder, borderline personality disorder, ADHD, dissociative identity disorder, and other conditions that can overlap or be misdiagnosed in trauma survivors. Requires a trauma-informed clinician who takes a full developmental and trauma history.
clinical.availability: Psychiatrists and clinical psychologists with trauma training. Many general practitioners and even general psychiatrists do not routinely take a detailed trauma history.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Anxiety disorders (particularly generalised anxiety, panic disorder, and social anxiety) co-occur with PTSD at high rates. Both involve dysregulated threat-detection systems. In trauma survivors, anxiety often represents chronic hyperarousal rather than a primary anxiety disorder, and treating only the anxiety without addressing the trauma typically produces limited results.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Major depressive disorder co-occurs in 50–70% of people with PTSD. Chronic trauma exposure, disrupted HPA axis function, neuroinflammation, and the toll of avoidance and isolation all contribute. Depression in trauma survivors often includes strong shame, worthlessness, and anhedonia, and may not respond adequately to antidepressants alone without trauma-focused treatment.

related_conditions.name: IBS
related_conditions.explanation: PTSD significantly increases the risk of IBS and functional gut disorders. The gut-brain axis is dysregulated by chronic stress and trauma, altering gut motility, visceral sensitivity, and the gut microbiome. Studies show PTSD is overrepresented in IBS populations and vice versa. Gut symptoms may be directly trauma-triggered.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: PTSD and fibromyalgia share significant overlap in mechanisms — central sensitisation, HPA axis dysregulation, and neuroinflammation — and frequently co-occur. Trauma history is common in fibromyalgia populations. The two conditions may share a common pathway through chronic stress-induced neurobiological changes.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic trauma and PTSD can drive central sensitisation — a state in which the central nervous system becomes amplified in its pain and threat processing. This explains why many PTSD survivors develop widespread pain, sensory hypersensitivity, and hypersensitivity to environmental triggers.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: PTSD profoundly disrupts autonomic nervous system regulation, and dysautonomia is increasingly recognised as a consequence of chronic trauma. Symptoms such as rapid heart rate, dizziness on standing, and exercise intolerance may reflect autonomic dysregulation with a trauma component.

related_conditions.name: ADHD
related_conditions.explanation: PTSD and ADHD share overlapping symptoms — difficulty concentrating, impulsivity, emotional dysregulation, hyperactivity, and sleep problems — leading to frequent misdiagnosis in both directions. Childhood trauma in particular can produce a clinical picture that closely resembles ADHD. Some individuals have both conditions. Distinguishing them requires a thorough trauma history.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Emerging evidence suggests that chronic stress and trauma can activate mast cells via neuroinflammatory pathways, and MCAS is increasingly recognised in complex trauma populations. The overlap between PTSD-related sensory hypersensitivity and MCAS symptoms is an active area of investigation.

related_conditions.name: hEDS / HSD
related_conditions.explanation: Hypermobile EDS and related connective tissue disorders appear to co-occur with PTSD and anxiety at higher rates than expected by chance. Some researchers suggest shared neurobiological pathways (e.g. noradrenergic dysregulation) that increase vulnerability to both conditions. The chronic pain and disability of hEDS can itself be traumatising.

related_conditions.name: Endometriosis
related_conditions.explanation: People with endometriosis have significantly elevated rates of PTSD, which may reflect both the trauma of living with a painful, often dismissed chronic illness and the impact of chronic pain on the nervous system. The relationship between trauma, central sensitisation, and endometriosis severity is an emerging area of research.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic stress and trauma alter the gut microbiome through neuroendocrine pathways, reducing microbial diversity and shifting microbial composition. Dysbiosis in turn amplifies neuroinflammation and HPA axis reactivity, potentially maintaining PTSD symptoms. The gut-brain axis is bidirectional in trauma.

related_conditions.name: Thyroid dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic HPA axis dysregulation from PTSD can affect thyroid function. Thyroid dysfunction (both hypo- and hyperthyroidism) shares symptoms with PTSD including fatigue, mood disturbance, cognitive fog, and sleep disruption, and should be ruled out in complex presentations.

related_conditions.name: Pelvic floor dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Trauma — especially sexual trauma, but also generalised threat-state — can cause chronic pelvic floor muscle tension and dysfunction. The pelvic floor often holds trauma in the body. Pelvic floor dysfunction may present with pelvic pain, urinary urgency, or sexual dysfunction and may require trauma-informed physiotherapy.

related_conditions.name: Borderline personality disorder (BPD)
related_conditions.explanation: C-PTSD and BPD share many features — emotional dysregulation, unstable relationships, fear of abandonment, self-harm, and identity disturbance — leading to frequent misdiagnosis. The ICD-11 recognises C-PTSD as distinct from BPD. Many people diagnosed with BPD have significant trauma histories, and the label has historically been applied without trauma being explored. The treatment implications differ significantly.

related_conditions.name: Dissociative identity disorder (DID)
related_conditions.explanation: DID lies at the extreme end of the dissociative spectrum and typically develops from severe, prolonged childhood trauma. It exists on a continuum with C-PTSD. Many people with DID are initially diagnosed with PTSD or other conditions. Both require trauma-informed treatment, but DID requires specific expertise.

related_conditions.name: Substance use disorders
related_conditions.explanation: Alcohol, cannabis, opioids, and stimulants are frequently used by PTSD survivors to manage hyperarousal, emotional pain, and sleep disturbance. Comorbid substance use is present in 35–50% of people seeking treatment for PTSD. Integrated treatment addressing both conditions simultaneously produces better outcomes than treating either in isolation.

related_conditions.name: Chronic fatigue syndrome / ME
related_conditions.explanation: Trauma and PTSD are significantly overrepresented in ME/CFS populations. Shared mechanisms include HPA axis dysregulation, neuroinflammation, and autonomic dysfunction. The boundary between the two is not always clear, and many people with ME/CFS have unaddressed trauma histories.

related_conditions.name: Eating disorders
related_conditions.explanation: PTSD — particularly interpersonal trauma — substantially increases the risk of eating disorders, including anorexia, bulimia, and binge-eating disorder. Disordered eating may function as a way to regulate emotions, manage dissociation, or assert control. Trauma-informed eating disorder treatment is needed.

strategies.name: Trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy (TF-CBT / CPT / PE)
strategies.description: Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT) and Prolonged Exposure (PE) are the most extensively researched trauma-focused therapies. CPT works by identifying and challenging unhelpful beliefs that emerged from the trauma (stuck points). PE involves gradual, structured exposure to trauma memories and reminders to reduce avoidance and desensitise the fear response. Both require a trained therapist and typically involve 8–16 sessions.
strategies.notes: These are first-line recommended treatments in most clinical guidelines. They can feel demanding and temporarily increase distress during processing. Not appropriate for everyone — particularly those with severe dissociation, active substance use, or insufficient safety and stability — without modification.

strategies.name: EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitisation and Reprocessing)
strategies.description: EMDR uses bilateral sensory stimulation (typically eye movements, but also tapping or sound) while the person holds elements of the traumatic memory in mind. The mechanism is not fully understood but may involve disrupting the reconsolidation of traumatic memories and reducing their vividness and emotional charge. Widely used and recommended in international guidelines.
strategies.notes: Particularly useful for discrete trauma memories. Some individuals process trauma more rapidly with EMDR than with verbal therapies. Requires a certified EMDR therapist. Can be adapted for C-PTSD with an extended stabilisation phase.

strategies.name: Somatic and body-based approaches
strategies.description: Approaches such as Somatic Experiencing (SE), Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, and the Polyvagal-informed therapy address trauma stored in the body through awareness of bodily sensations, movement, and nervous system states — rather than primarily through narrative or cognitive processing. Particularly useful where verbal processing is limited or where somatic symptoms are prominent.
strategies.notes: Evidence base is growing but less extensive than for CPT, PE, or EMDR. Many trauma survivors find body-based approaches transformative, particularly those with dissociation or alexithymia (difficulty identifying emotions). Requires a specifically trained therapist.

strategies.name: Stabilisation and window of tolerance work
strategies.description: Before trauma processing can begin safely, many people — particularly those with C-PTSD or ongoing life stressors — need to build the capacity to stay within their 'window of tolerance' (the zone of arousal in which processing is possible). Stabilisation includes grounding techniques, distress tolerance skills, and developing internal resources.
strategies.notes: Not a standalone treatment for PTSD, but an essential phase for many people. Skipping stabilisation and moving straight to trauma processing can be destabilising, particularly in complex cases. Phase-based treatment (stabilise, process, integrate) is considered best practice for C-PTSD.

strategies.name: Nervous system regulation practices
strategies.description: Practices that activate the parasympathetic nervous system can reduce chronic hyperarousal and build regulatory capacity over time. These include diaphragmatic breathing, extended exhale breathing (physiological sigh), vagal toning exercises, cold water exposure, and humming or singing (which vibrate the vagus nerve). Yoga, qigong, and tai chi have evidence specifically in PTSD populations.
strategies.notes: These are adjuncts to — not replacements for — trauma-focused therapy. Trauma-sensitive yoga in particular has shown promise in clinical trials with trauma survivors. Regular practice over weeks to months appears necessary for durable change.

strategies.name: SSRI / SNRI medication
strategies.description: Sertraline, paroxetine (SSRIs), and venlafaxine (SNRI) are the medications with the strongest evidence base for PTSD symptom reduction and are FDA-approved for PTSD. They can reduce hyperarousal, nightmares, and depressive symptoms, and may make engagement with therapy more possible.
strategies.notes: Medication alone rarely resolves PTSD and is generally considered most useful as an adjunct to trauma-focused therapy. Response rates are modest compared to therapy. Prazosin has evidence specifically for PTSD-related nightmares. Should be managed by a prescriber familiar with trauma, ideally a psychiatrist.

strategies.name: MDMA-assisted psychotherapy (clinical trials)
strategies.description: MDMA-assisted therapy, delivered in carefully controlled clinical settings with trained therapists, has shown striking efficacy in Phase 2 and Phase 3 trials, particularly for treatment-resistant PTSD. MDMA is thought to temporarily reduce amygdala reactivity and increase social trust, allowing deeper trauma processing than is possible in ordinary therapeutic states.
strategies.notes: Not yet approved for routine clinical use (as of 2025, FDA review is ongoing following initial rejection). Available only through clinical trials or specialist centres in some jurisdictions. Not appropriate for people with certain cardiovascular or psychiatric conditions.

strategies.name: Sleep hygiene and nightmare management
strategies.description: Given how central sleep disruption is to PTSD severity, targeted sleep support can reduce the overall symptom burden. Image Rehearsal Therapy (IRT) — a technique in which the person consciously rewrites the ending of recurring nightmares while awake and rehearses the new version — has good evidence for reducing nightmare frequency.
strategies.notes: IRT requires some instruction, often from a therapist familiar with the technique. Basic sleep hygiene (regular schedule, dark room, avoiding screens and stimulants) remains foundational.

strategies.name: Social support and safe relational connection
strategies.description: Safe, consistent social connection — whether through relationships, support groups, peer communities, or group therapy — directly supports nervous system co-regulation. Humans are biological social animals and recover from trauma more effectively in safe relational contexts than in isolation.
strategies.notes: C-PTSD in particular often disrupts the capacity to access relational support. Trauma-focused group therapy can be powerful precisely because it addresses interpersonal trauma in a relational context. Forced social situations or unsafe relationships do not help and may re-traumatise.

strategies.name: Physical exercise
strategies.description: Aerobic exercise has evidence for reducing PTSD severity, improving sleep, reducing depression, and supporting neuroplasticity (including hippocampal volume, which is often reduced in PTSD). Trauma-sensitive movement practices (yoga, walking, martial arts) can also reconnect people with their bodies in a safe way.
strategies.notes: Exercise intensity may need to be adapted — high-intensity exercise can activate the same physiological state as trauma triggers for some people. Starting with lower-intensity, rhythmic, predictable movement is often better tolerated initially.

things_to_avoid: Avoiding all trauma reminders indefinitely — avoidance maintains and extends PTSD by preventing the brain from learning that the memory is not the same as the event
Alcohol and sedatives as the primary means of managing distress — they impair sleep quality, impair trauma processing, and increase the risk of dependence
Trauma processing therapy without a stabilisation phase when dissociation is prominent — this can lead to destabilisation, flooding, and dropout from treatment
Generic counselling or supportive talking therapy without trauma-specific training — it may provide support but is unlikely to resolve core PTSD symptoms and may inadvertently reinforce avoidance

Pushing through exposure without adequate coping tools — gradual, titrated approach ('pendulation' between distress and safety) is more effective and less harmful than forcing exposure before the person is ready
Isolating as a coping strategy — although understandable, prolonged isolation maintains hyperarousal and removes the co-regulatory benefits of safe social connection
Minimising or normalising the original trauma ('it wasn't that bad', 'others had it worse') — this maintains shame and blocks processing
Expecting a linear recovery — PTSD recovery typically involves periods of improvement, setbacks, and reopening of previously worked-through material, particularly at life transitions

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Talking about trauma in open-ended, unstructured ways without a trained trauma therapist often does not help and can make things worse. Ventilating the story without processing it can reinforce and deepen the traumatic memory network.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Sharing the story feels cathartic and is socially encouraged. Many well-meaning people (and untrained therapists) believe that 'getting it out' is the same as healing. People often mistake emotional release during retelling for therapeutic progress.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Working with a therapist specifically trained in structured, trauma-focused methods (EMDR, CPT, PE, somatic therapy) — who guides titrated processing rather than unstructured retelling — produces measurable, durable symptom reduction in most people who complete treatment.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: The nervous system does not care about intellectual insight. Understanding why PTSD developed, or knowing cognitively that the trauma is over, does not resolve the body's threat response. PTSD lives in implicit memory and physiology, not in narrative understanding.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Insight feels like progress and is highly valued. People invest heavily in understanding their trauma history. The idea that knowing is not enough is deeply frustrating, especially for people who are highly self-aware.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Prioritising body-based, processing-focused interventions rather than purely narrative or analytical ones. Many people find that progress accelerates significantly once they shift from understanding the trauma to reprocessing it at a physiological level.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: PTSD recovery typically requires directly engaging with the traumatic material at some point — there is no lasting resolution through avoidance alone, no matter how sophisticated the coping strategies become.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Approaching traumatic memories feels genuinely dangerous. Avoidance reduces distress in the short term, making it strongly reinforcing. Many people have been managing for years and fear that opening the trauma will be permanently destabilising.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: With an appropriately skilled therapist, adequate stabilisation, and titrated pacing, the majority of people who engage with trauma processing report that it is less overwhelming than they feared and that their overall functioning improves significantly.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Many physical symptoms experienced by people with PTSD — chronic pain, fatigue, gut problems, autoimmune flares — have a significant trauma-driven neurobiological component that will not fully resolve through medical treatment alone, however many physical investigations are done.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Physical symptoms feel physical and unambiguously real. The idea that they might have a trauma component can feel like being told it is 'all in the head' — which it is not. The neurobiological changes in PTSD are measurable and physiological, not imagined, but this is poorly communicated by medical systems.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who pursue trauma treatment alongside medical management of physical symptoms typically report better overall outcomes than those who pursue physical treatment alone. The body responds to trauma resolution.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Most people with PTSD are not diagnosed for years — sometimes decades. Many have seen multiple clinicians who treated the symptoms (depression, anxiety, chronic pain, substance use) without ever asking about or identifying the trauma.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: This feels like a systemic failure that is both angering and demoralising. It is. Acknowledging it requires sitting with the fact that one might have suffered longer than necessary, which can be hard to accept.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Finding a trauma-trained clinician — not just any mental health professional — changes the treatment trajectory. Many people report that the first time a clinician took their trauma history seriously and named what had happened was itself a significant turning point.

key_levers.lever: Finding a therapist with specific, advanced training in trauma-focused methods
key_levers.why_it_matters: The evidence clearly shows that PTSD responds to specific interventions (EMDR, CPT, PE, somatic therapies) that most generalist therapists are not trained to deliver. Seeing any therapist — without this training — often produces years of supportive but non-curative work. Matching to the right treatment modality is arguably the single most important factor in recovery.
key_levers.how_to_start: Search specifically for therapists credentialed in EMDR (EMDR International Association), certified in CPT, or trained in somatic trauma therapies. Ask directly: 'What trauma-specific training do you have?' before booking. Trauma-focused therapists exist in private practice, specialist trauma services, and veteran/survivor organisations.
key_levers.timeline: Most people completing a full course of trauma-focused therapy (8–20 sessions) experience significant symptom reduction. Some require longer, particularly with C-PTSD.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Trauma processing is not possible when someone is in ongoing danger, severe instability, or crisis. External safety (leaving an abusive situation, housing, financial stability) and internal safety (basic emotion regulation capacity, reduced substance use, a minimal network of support) are prerequisites for trauma therapy to work. Attempting processing without safety often leads to decompensation and treatment dropout.
key_levers.how_to_start: Assess current safety honestly. Work with a therapist or support service on stabilisation first. Address active crises — domestic violence, homelessness, active severe substance use — in parallel with beginning trauma-informed support.
key_levers.timeline: Stabilisation may take weeks to months before active trauma processing can begin, depending on circumstances. This is not delay — it is necessary foundation-building.

key_levers.why_it_matters: The HPA axis and autonomic nervous system can be directly shaped through regular practice. Building a daily nervous system regulation practice — even 10–20 minutes — over months creates a measurable shift in baseline arousal, resilience to triggers, and capacity to engage with therapy. This is not about 'calming down' but about rewiring default threat-state calibration.
key_levers.how_to_start: Identify one accessible practice — diaphragmatic breathing, extended exhale breathing, trauma-sensitive yoga, or cold water exposure — and begin daily. Track how it affects baseline mood and reactivity over 4–6 weeks.
key_levers.timeline: Noticeable shift in baseline arousal typically within 4–8 weeks of consistent daily practice; more significant neurobiological changes require 3–6 months.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Every avoidance behaviour — no matter how understandable — signals to the nervous system that the avoided thing is genuinely dangerous, maintaining and often enlarging the scope of PTSD. Gradual, supported approach towards avoided situations is necessary for recovery.
key_levers.how_to_start: With therapist support, build a hierarchy of avoided situations from least to most distressing. Begin with the least distressing and work upward. Do not attempt this alone or rapidly — pacing matters.
key_levers.timeline: Measurable reduction in avoidance-driven life restriction is often visible within weeks to months of systematic exposure work.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Sleep is when memory consolidation and emotional processing occur. Chronic sleep disruption from nightmares and hyperarousal perpetuates trauma memory dysregulation, emotional lability, and cognitive impairment. Improving sleep quality has wide-ranging downstream effects on PTSD severity.
key_levers.how_to_start: Implement sleep hygiene basics. Consider Image Rehearsal Therapy for recurring nightmares (a therapist can guide this). Discuss medication options for severe nightmares with a prescriber (prazosin has evidence). Evaluate caffeine and alcohol timing.
key_levers.timeline: Sleep improvements from IRT typically emerge within 2–4 weeks of practice.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Humans recover from trauma within safe relationships. Safe, consistent connection with others who understand — whether through peer support groups, group therapy, or survivor communities — directly supports nervous system co-regulation and reduces the shame and isolation that maintain PTSD.
key_levers.how_to_start: Identify one community — an in-person or online peer support group specific to the type of trauma experienced, or a group therapy programme — and make contact. Start with lower-risk formats (online, reading others' experiences) if in-person connection feels too exposing initially.
key_levers.timeline: Benefits of peer connection tend to build gradually over weeks to months of consistent engagement.

common_mistakes: Choosing the nearest available therapist rather than one specifically trained in trauma — this often results in years of supportive but non-curative work and can inadvertently reinforce avoidance
Expecting to process trauma before building adequate regulation and stabilisation capacity — leads to overwhelm, flooding, and sometimes retreat from treatment altogether
Using alcohol, cannabis, or other substances to manage PTSD symptoms — provides short-term relief but impairs sleep quality, undermines processing, and often escalates over time
Treating only the downstream diagnoses (depression, IBS, chronic pain, anxiety) while the trauma driving them remains unaddressed — produces partial and often temporary improvement

Interpreting temporary worsening during trauma processing as evidence that the therapy is harmful — some increase in distress during active processing is normal and expected; it does not mean the approach is wrong
Attempting to 'think through' or 'intellectualise' recovery without engaging with the physiological and emotional dimensions of trauma — yields insight without resolution

Stopping therapy prematurely when early gains are made — substantial gains are possible in a short time, but consolidation and addressing deeper layers typically requires continuing beyond initial symptom relief

Expecting a straight line of recovery and measuring progress against pre-trauma functioning rather than against where one was a year ago — recovery is non-linear and irregular, and comparing to pre-trauma baseline can be demoralising and misleading

specialists.type: Trauma-specialist psychologist or psychotherapist (EMDR, CPT, PE trained)
specialists.role: The most critical practitioner for the majority of people with PTSD. Delivers the specific, evidence-based interventions that resolve PTSD rather than simply managing it. Look for specific credentials: EMDR International Association certification, CPT certification, or advanced training in somatic trauma therapies.
specialists.notes: Not all psychologists or therapists have trauma-specific training. Asking directly about their trauma training and approach before booking is appropriate and important. A general counsellor or CBT therapist without trauma specialisation is unlikely to be sufficient for moderate to severe PTSD.

specialists.role: Provides diagnostic clarity, manages medication (SSRIs, prazosin for nightmares), and can coordinate complex care where PTSD overlaps with other psychiatric conditions. A trauma-informed psychiatrist is one who takes a thorough trauma history and understands trauma's role in the overall presentation.
specialists.notes: Many psychiatrists are primarily medication-focused and do not offer trauma therapy themselves. A psychiatrist who understands trauma can still be invaluable for medication management and differential diagnosis, particularly in complex presentations.

specialists.role: Addresses the physiological and bodily dimensions of trauma — particularly useful where verbal processing is limited, where somatic symptoms are prominent, or where the person has tried talk-based approaches without success.
specialists.notes: Relatively less common than EMDR or CBT therapists. Somatic Experiencing practitioners are certified through the Somatic Experiencing International organisation. Sensorimotor Psychotherapy practitioners have their own certification track.

specialists.type: Trauma-informed GP or integrative medicine physician
specialists.role: Can provide initial screening, referral to appropriate services, and address physical comorbidities. A trauma-informed GP will take a trauma history as part of evaluating complex, persistent, or unexplained health problems.
specialists.notes: Many GPs do not routinely screen for PTSD or take detailed trauma histories. Finding a GP who does — or who is willing to engage with the trauma model — can significantly improve navigation of the healthcare system.

specialists.role: For those with PTSD-related pelvic floor tension, chronic pelvic pain, or sexual dysfunction, a physiotherapist trained in trauma-informed pelvic care can address the physical holding patterns that often accompany trauma.
specialists.notes: Standard physiotherapy without trauma-informed training may inadvertently re-traumatise through physical interventions. Look specifically for trauma-informed pelvic floor practitioners.

specialists.type: Social worker or trauma-informed case worker
specialists.role: Essential for navigating access to services, particularly for those dealing with financial hardship, housing instability, domestic violence, or systemic barriers to care. Practical stabilisation is a prerequisite for trauma therapy.
specialists.notes: Often overlooked but critical for people whose trauma is ongoing or whose circumstances are unstable. Many trauma-focused organisations combine therapy and practical support.

patient_communities.name: r/PTSD
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community for people with PTSD. Rules-based to maintain safety. Useful for peer support, shared experience, and practical information about treatment options and finding therapists.

patient_communities.name: r/CPTSD
patient_communities.notes: Specifically focused on complex PTSD. One of the most active and well-moderated trauma communities online. Strong culture of peer support and self-directed education. Particularly valuable for those whose trauma is developmental or relational.

patient_communities.name: After Silence
patient_communities.notes: Peer support community specifically for survivors of rape and sexual assault. Moderated, safe, and long-established.

patient_communities.name: PTSD UK
patient_communities.notes: UK-based charity providing information, peer support, and signposting to services for PTSD and C-PTSD. Includes a community support group directory.

patient_communities.name: Gift From Within
patient_communities.notes: Long-running peer support organisation for PTSD survivors, with particular focus on first responders, military, and disaster survivors. Articles, survivor stories, and community resources.

learning_resources.title: The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma
learning_resources.description: The most widely read book on trauma and its physical impact. Explains how trauma reshapes the brain and body, why talk therapy alone is often insufficient, and what body-based and experiential approaches can do. Accessible to non-specialists. Essential reading for anyone trying to understand why their body reacts the way it does.

learning_resources.title: Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving
learning_resources.description: A practical, compassionate guide to C-PTSD written by a therapist who is himself a trauma survivor. Covers emotional flashbacks, the inner critic, the fawn response, and practical tools for recovery. Particularly valuable for those with developmental or relational trauma who recognise themselves in the C-PTSD description but may not have had a formal diagnosis.

learning_resources.title: Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma
learning_resources.description: The foundational text on Somatic Experiencing, explaining how trauma is held in the body and how physiological completion of the trauma response can bring resolution. Accessible and includes practical exercises. A valuable introduction to somatic approaches.

learning_resources.title: What My Bones Know: A Memoir of Healing from Complex Trauma
learning_resources.description: A journalist's account of discovering her C-PTSD diagnosis and navigating the treatment landscape. Combines memoir with thorough investigation of the science and treatment options. Particularly good at conveying what C-PTSD actually feels like from the inside, and at demystifying the treatment process.

learning_resources.title: The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy
learning_resources.description: An accessible explanation of Polyvagal Theory — the science of how the autonomic nervous system shapes safety, connection, and trauma responses — and how it applies to therapeutic work. Helps make sense of why the nervous system does what it does and what regulation actually means.

learning_resources.title: Trauma-Informed Care Implementation Resource Center
learning_resources.description: A comprehensive professional and patient resource on trauma-informed care principles, research, and implementation. Useful for understanding what trauma-informed care means and what to look for — and ask for — from healthcare providers.

learning_resources.title: National Center for PTSD
learning_resources.description: The most comprehensive publicly available evidence-based resource on PTSD. Includes information on diagnosis, treatment options, self-help tools (including the PTSD Coach app), and a treatment provider locator. Well-referenced and regularly updated. Not only for veterans — the information is widely applicable.

learning_resources.title: PTSD Coach App
learning_resources.description: A free, evidence-informed app for managing PTSD symptoms between therapy sessions. Includes psychoeducation, symptom tracking, coping tools, and crisis contacts. Designed for use alongside — not instead of — therapy.

learning_resources.title: Recovering from Trauma (TED Talk)
learning_resources.description: TED's collection of trauma-related talks covers neuroscience, recovery approaches, and personal accounts. A good entry point for people who prefer video to reading. Bessel van der Kolk, Peter Levine, and others have given accessible talks on trauma and healing.

learning_resources.title: Healing Trauma: A Pioneering Program for Restoring the Wisdom of Your Body
learning_resources.description: A shorter, more practical companion to 'Waking the Tiger', including guided exercises for working with the body's trauma responses at home. Useful for those who want to begin somatic self-work while seeking a therapist.

learning_resources.title: ICD-11 Clinical Descriptions and Diagnostic Guidelines for C-PTSD
learning_resources.author_or_source: World Health Organization / Cloitre et al.
learning_resources.description: For those who want to understand the formal diagnostic criteria for C-PTSD as recognised in ICD-11 — useful when seeking diagnosis or explaining the distinction between PTSD and C-PTSD to clinicians who may not be familiar with the ICD-11 category.

sections.relevance_signal: **History of significant trauma** — childhood adversity, abuse, major loss — and IBS that has never responded to dietary or medication approaches. Gut symptoms are treatment-resistant.
